terms.def: Conditional VaR = Expected Shortfall: the average loss in the worst α% of scenarios; always ≥ VaR.
Value at Risk: the loss not exceeded with probability (1−α); e.g. 99% VaR says 1% of days are worse than this.
Risk from extreme, low-probability events in the left tail of the P&L distribution.
A risk measure satisfying sub-additivity, monotonicity, positive homogeneity, and translation invariance. CVaR is coherent; VaR is not.
Risk(A+B) ≤ Risk(A) + Risk(B); diversification never increases risk — a property VaR can violate.
The probability distribution of portfolio losses over a horizon; CVaR and VaR are quantiles/tails of this distribution.

eli3: Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) — also called Expected Shortfall (ES) or Expected Tail Loss — answers a question VaR ignores: 'When things go badly, how bad do they actually get?' CVaR is the average loss in the worst alpha percent of scenarios. If 1-day 99% VaR is $1M, CVaR tells you the average loss on those worst 1% of days — which might be $3M or $20M depending on how fat the tail is.

CVaR is a better risk measure than VaR for three reasons: it captures the entire tail (not just a threshold); it's sub-additive (diversification always reduces CVaR); and it can't be gamed by strategies that have small VaR but catastrophic tail losses. Regulators (Basel FRTB) switched from VaR to ES in 2016 for these reasons.

Intuition: VaR is like a pass/fail test ('did you exceed the threshold?'); CVaR is like a grade ('how bad was it when you failed?'). Both are important, but CVaR gives more information about tail risk — particularly important for strategies with asymmetric return profiles.

whenWhy.title: Computing CVaR
whenWhy.body: CVaR at level alpha is the average loss across all scenarios where the loss exceeds the corresponding VaR. For a 99% CVaR with N=1000 historical scenarios: sort P&L from worst to best, then average the worst 10 observations (the worst 1%). CVaR is always at least as large as VaR; the gap reflects tail severity. For a normal distribution, CVaR at 99% is roughly 2.67 sigma versus VaR at 99% which is 2.33 sigma. For fat-tailed distributions, CVaR can be 2 to 5 times larger than VaR.

whenWhy.title: CVaR optimisation
whenWhy.body: CVaR is a coherent risk measure and a convex function of portfolio weights, meaning you can directly minimise CVaR in a portfolio optimisation problem. Rockafellar & Uryasev (2000) showed this can be reformulated as a linear program: introduce one auxiliary variable per scenario and solve a linear program over scenario outcomes. The result is a portfolio that naturally avoids tail-risk strategies — unlike mean-variance optimisation which treats upside and downside symmetrically, CVaR minimisation only penalises downside tail risk.

whenWhy.title: CVaR in tail risk hedging
whenWhy.body: CVaR provides a principled framework for sizing tail risk hedges. Adding put options reduces CVaR by capping losses in tail scenarios. Each position's contribution to expected tail loss — its average loss conditional on the portfolio being in the tail — guides hedging decisions. Positions with the highest CVaR contribution should be shorted or hedged first. This is analogous to factor risk attribution but for tail risk, and is used by hedge funds and risk parity funds to identify which positions dominate in stress scenarios.

deepDiveIntro: A risk measure is called coherent (Artzner et al. 1999) if it satisfies four properties: monotonicity (riskier portfolios get higher risk numbers), sub-additivity (combined risk never exceeds the sum of individual risks), positive homogeneity (doubling the portfolio doubles the risk), and translation invariance (adding cash reduces risk by the cash amount). VaR fails sub-additivity for non-elliptical distributions — two portfolios can each have a moderate VaR but their combined portfolio can have a much larger VaR. CVaR satisfies all four properties, making it preferable for regulatory and risk management purposes.

CVaR can also be expressed as the worst-case expected loss over a class of probability measures that put at most 1/alpha weight on any individual scenario. This dual representation connects CVaR to robust optimisation and makes it useful for stress testing frameworks.

deepDive.heading: CVaR vs VaR: Tail Risk Strategy Comparison
deepDive.body: Consider two strategies: Strategy A earns +0.5% daily on 99% of days and loses -3% on the remaining 1%. Strategy B earns +0.3% daily on 99% of days, loses -50% on 0.9% of days, and loses -1% on 0.1% of days. Strategy A: both VaR and CVaR at 99% are -3%. Strategy B: VaR at 99% is only -1% (only 1% of days exceed this threshold, and most of those are in the -1% bucket), but CVaR is around -45%. VaR massively favours Strategy B — it appears much safer. CVaR correctly identifies Strategy B as catastrophic in the tail. Selling out-of-the-money options (similar to Strategy B) looks safe on a VaR basis until the tail event occurs. CVaR-based risk limits would correctly penalise such strategies. [1]

deepDive.heading: CVaR Estimation in Practice: Sample Size and Accuracy
deepDive.body: CVaR estimation requires a large sample because it averages only the tail observations. For 99% CVaR with 250 daily observations, only 2 or 3 observations contribute to the estimate — extremely noisy. For reliable estimation you need thousands of observations. Solutions: (1) Fit a parametric tail distribution (Generalised Pareto Distribution, or GPD) to the extremes and compute CVaR analytically. (2) Monte Carlo simulation with a calibrated model: generating 100,000 scenarios produces a much more stable CVaR estimate. (3) Importance sampling: oversample the tail region and reweight to get an unbiased estimate with fewer simulations. For regulatory purposes, Basel FRTB uses Expected Shortfall at 97.5% rather than 99%, which requires less extrapolation. [2]

deepDive.heading: Spectral Risk Measures and CVaR as a Special Case
deepDive.body: CVaR is a special case of spectral risk measures, which assign a weight to each quantile of the loss distribution. CVaR gives equal weight to all scenarios in the worst alpha fraction of outcomes and zero weight to the rest. More general spectral measures allow custom weight functions: a risk manager who cares more about catastrophic losses than moderate losses can use a weight function that decreases steeply in the tail. The Wang transform, used in insurance pricing, applies this idea to adjust risk probabilities in a way consistent with market pricing. For most asset management applications, standard CVaR (equal weight on the tail) is sufficient; more sophisticated weight functions are used in insurance and structured products. [1]

takeaways: CVaR_α = average loss in worst α% of scenarios; always ≥ VaR; captures tail severity, not just threshold. [1]
CVaR is coherent (sub-additive): diversification always reduces CVaR; VaR can violate this. [1]
CVaR optimisation: Rockafellar-Uryasev reformulation as LP — minimise CVaR directly in portfolio construction. [1]
CVaR estimation requires large samples; use parametric tail fit (GPD) or MC with importance sampling. [2]
Basel FRTB 2016: replaced VaR with ES at 97.5% for bank market risk capital — CVaR captures tail better. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does CVaR behave under stress scenario analysis?
edgeCases.a: CVaR is naturally suited to stress testing because it already focuses on the tail. When you add specific stress scenarios to the historical simulation, they directly contribute to CVaR (if they're worse than the VaR threshold). Conditional CVaR by scenario type: 'What is our CVaR conditional on a 2008-style credit crisis?' — select only the stress scenarios that match 2008-style conditions; compute CVaR from this subset. CVaR decomposition by risk factor: 'What fraction of our CVaR comes from equity market risk vs. credit risk vs. rates?' — compute CVaR contribution of each factor position. This allows risk managers to identify which risks dominate the tail, regardless of whether they dominate in normal times (which is the VaR decomposition). Example: a portfolio might have interest rate risk as 60% of daily VaR but only 20% of CVaR (because equity crashes dominate the tail scenarios).

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between CVaR and stressed CVaR?
edgeCases.a: CVaR uses the historical distribution over the standard risk window (typically 1-3 years for most risk systems). Stressed CVaR uses the historical distribution from the worst 12-month window in history (identified as the period where the bank's current portfolio would have had the highest losses). In Basel III (before FRTB): capital = VaR + Stressed VaR (using worst historical 12-month period). The stressed VaR was introduced because banks' VaR models used 2003-2006 data (calm period) and drastically underestimated the 2008 crisis — the VaR didn't reflect the kind of market stress that was actually possible. Under FRTB: stressed ES (SES) plays the same role — computed over the single worst 12-month period identified for the current portfolio. The selection of the stressed period is done by identifying the 12-month window that maximises the portfolio's ES, not just any historical crisis.

sources.label: Artzner, P. et al. (1999) — Coherent Measures of Risk, Mathematical Finance
Rockafellar, R.T. & Uryasev, S. (2000) — Optimization of Conditional Value-at-Risk, Journal of Risk
